metadata.deck_subtitle: Birthday Party — Let's Go to a Party!
metadata.description: Vocabulary from Integrated Chinese Lesson 14 Dialogue I: invitations, gifts to bring (drinks, fruit, flowers), measure words, and arranging a ride.

content.name: dance_party
content.side_e: Wǎnshang wǒmen zài tā jiā kāi wǔhuì.
content.side_f: Tonight we're throwing a dance party at her place.
content.side_g: 舞 means 'dance' and 会 means 'gathering/meeting,' so 舞会 is literally a 'dancing gathering.' Use 开舞会 for 'throw a dance party,' 去舞会 for 'go to a dance party.' Distinct from 派对 (pàiduì, casual party loanword) — 舞会 implies dancing is the point. Also appears in 生日舞会 (birthday dance party), 化装舞会 (masquerade ball).

content.name: older_female_cousin
content.side_a: Older female cousin (mother's side, or father's sister's daughter)
content.side_e: Zhè shì wǒ biǎojiě, tā bǐ wǒ dà liǎng suì.
content.side_f: This is my older female cousin; she's two years older than me.
content.side_g: 表 marks cousins through your mother or your father's sister; 堂 (táng) marks cousins through your father's brother. 姐 = older sister, so 表姐 is specifically an older female cousin. Other forms: 表妹 (younger), 表哥 (older male), 表弟 (younger male). Chinese kinship is precise — there is no generic 'cousin' word.

content.name: middle_school
content.side_e: Tā shì wǒ de zhōngxué tóngxué.
content.side_g: 中 means 'middle' and 学 is short for 学校 (school). 中学 covers grades 7-12 in China — initial half is 初中 (chūzhōng, junior high) and the upper half is 高中 (gāozhōng, senior high). Compare with 小学 (xiǎoxué, elementary) and 大学 (dàxué, college/university). 中学同学 means 'middle-school classmate,' a common way to introduce old friends.

content.name: to_give_as_gift
content.side_a: To give (as a gift); to send; to deliver
content.side_e: Wǒ mǎi le yì běn shū sòng gěi tā.
content.side_f: I bought a book to give to her.
content.side_g: 送 covers giving (a gift), sending (a delivery), and seeing someone off. Pattern: 送 + recipient (with 给) + item, e.g. 送给她一本书 or 送一本书给她. Different from 给 (gěi, to give plainly) — 送 implies generosity or ceremony. Also 送行 (see off), 送礼 (give a gift), 送货 (deliver goods).

content.name: gift
content.side_e: Nǐ yào sòng gěi Xiǎoyīn shénme shēngrì lǐwù?
content.side_f: What birthday gift are you giving Xiaoyin?
content.side_g: 礼 means 'courtesy/ceremony' and 物 means 'thing,' so 礼物 is literally a 'courtesy thing.' Pair with 送 (give) or 收 (receive): 送礼物, 收礼物. Specific kinds: 生日礼物 (birthday gift), 结婚礼物 (wedding gift), 圣诞礼物 (Christmas gift). Avoid 给 with 礼物 — use 送 for 'give a gift.'

content.name: measure_book
content.side_a: Measure word for books and bound items
content.side_e: Wǒ mǎi le yì běn shū hé sān běn zázhì.
content.side_f: I bought one book and three magazines.
content.side_g: 本 counts books, magazines, notebooks, dictionaries — anything bound. Pattern: number + 本 + noun, as in 一本书, 两本词典. Don't use 个 for books — use 本. As a separate word 本 also means 'this/own' (本人 = oneself, 本店 = this shop), but as a measure it's strictly bound items.

content.name: beverage
content.side_f: Drinks or fruit, either is fine.
content.side_g: 饮 means 'to drink' (literary) and 料 means 'material,' so 饮料 is the catch-all for non-alcoholic drinks — sodas, juices, sports drinks. For alcohol use 酒 (jiǔ); for hot drinks like tea/coffee, you'd say 茶 or 咖啡 directly. On menus 饮料 is the section header. 喝点饮料 = 'have a drink.'

content.name: fruit
content.side_g: 水 means 'water' and 果 means 'fruit,' so 水果 is literally 'watery fruit' — fresh fruit specifically. Dried fruit is 干果 (gānguǒ); nuts overlap with 坚果 (jiānguǒ). 吃水果 is the standard verb pairing. 水果店 = fruit shop, 水果汁 = fruit juice. Common gift to bring when visiting someone in China.

content.name: measure_bunch
content.side_a: Measure word for bunches, bouquets, and chairs
content.side_e: Wǒ zài mǎi yì bǎ huār.
content.side_f: I'll also buy a bouquet of flowers.
content.side_g: 把 counts things you can grasp by a handle — chairs (一把椅子), umbrellas (一把伞), knives (一把刀) — and bunches like flowers (一把花) or grass. As a verb 把 means 'to hold,' which is the etymological link. Don't confuse with the 把-construction grammar particle, which is a different use of the same character.

content.name: flower
content.side_e: Tā sòng le wǒ yì bǎ hěn piàoliang de huār.
content.side_f: He gave me a beautiful bouquet of flowers.
content.side_g: 花 is the standalone noun for flower; the diminutive 花儿 (huār) is common in northern speech. Measure words: 一朵花 (one bloom), 一束花 / 一把花 (a bouquet). 花 also appears as a verb meaning 'to spend' (money or time): 花钱, 花时间 — same character, different sense.

content.name: to_love
content.side_a: To love; to like; to be fond of
content.side_g: 爱 covers a wide affection range — 'love (a person)' and 'be fond of (an activity).' 爱 + verb means 'love doing,' as in 爱吃 (love eating), 爱看 (love watching). Stronger than 喜欢 (xǐhuan, like). For romantic love specifically, 爱情 (àiqíng) is the noun, 我爱你 the declaration. 最爱 = 'love most/most loved.'

content.name: apple
content.side_e: Wǒ zài mǎi yì xiē píngguǒ, lí hé xīguā ba.
content.side_f: I'll also buy some apples, pears, and watermelons.
content.side_g: 苹 (apple-tree) + 果 (fruit) = apple. Measure word is 个 (一个苹果) — apples don't take a special MW. Note 苹果公司 = Apple (the company) and 苹果手机 = iPhone. Distinct from 平 (píng, flat) which sounds the same — context disambiguates.

content.name: pear
content.side_e: Wǒ ài chī lí, bù tài ài chī píngguǒ.
content.side_f: I love pears, but don't really like apples.
content.side_g: 梨 is a single character for 'pear.' Note the homophone 离 (lí, 'leave/distance') — different character, same pronunciation. Cultural note: in Chinese custom, sharing a pear (分梨 fēn lí) sounds like 分离 (separation), so couples avoid splitting one. Common pairing: 雪梨 (snow pear), 梨汁 (pear juice).

content.name: watermelon
content.side_e: Xià tiān chī xīguā zuì shūfu.
content.side_f: Eating watermelon in summer is the most refreshing.
content.side_g: 西 means 'west' and 瓜 means 'melon/gourd' (the fruit reportedly came west to China via the Silk Road). Other 瓜 family: 黄瓜 (cucumber), 南瓜 (pumpkin), 冬瓜 (winter melon). Measure word: 一个西瓜 for whole, 一片西瓜 for a slice. Chinese summer staple, often served chilled.

content.name: to_live
content.side_a: To live (in a certain place); to reside
content.side_e: Nǐ zhù de dìfang lí Xiǎoyīn jiā hěn yuǎn.
content.side_f: Where you live is very far from Xiaoyin's house.
content.side_g: 住 = 'reside' (a place of dwelling), distinct from 生活 (shēnghuó, to live a life) and 活 (huó, to be alive). Pattern: 住在 + place — 我住在北京. Also as part of 住的地方 (the place [one] lives), and 住院 (be hospitalized). Don't use 住 for short stays — use 待 (dāi) or 留 (liú).

content.name: heavy
content.side_e: Shuǐguǒ hěn zhòng, wǒ kāi chē lái jiē nǐ.
content.side_f: The fruit is heavy, so I'll drive over to pick you up.
content.side_g: 重 covers physical weight ('heavy') and figurative weight ('serious, important'). 病很重 = the illness is serious; 任务很重 = the task is heavy. Opposite is 轻 (qīng, light). Note the second pronunciation: 重 (chóng) means 'again, repeat' — same character, different reading (重新 chóngxīn = anew).

content.name: to_pick_up
content.side_a: To pick (someone) up; to meet; to welcome
content.side_e: Wǒ kāi chē lái jiē nǐ, wǒmen yìqǐ qù ba.
content.side_f: I'll drive over to pick you up; let's go together.
content.side_g: 接 = meet someone arriving (at airport, train, home) and bring them along — opposite of 送 (see someone off). 去机场接朋友 = go to the airport to pick up a friend. Also extends to 'answer' (接电话 answer the phone) and 'connect' (接通). Don't confuse with 收 (shōu, receive a thing) — 接 is for receiving people or calls.

content.name: building_floor
content.side_a: Multi-story building; floor (of a multi-level building)
content.side_e: Wǒ liù diǎn bàn zài lóu xià děng nǐ.
content.side_f: I'll wait for you downstairs at six-thirty.
content.side_g: 楼 means both the whole building (一座楼 a building) and individual floors (三楼 third floor). Compounds: 楼上 (upstairs), 楼下 (downstairs), 教学楼 (teaching building), 公寓楼 (apartment building). Floor numbering matches American style — 一楼 = ground/first floor. For an unspecified floor use 层 (céng) instead.

content.name: wang_hong
content.side_e: Tīngshuō hái yǒu Xiǎoyīn de zhōngxué tóngxué Wáng Hóng.
content.side_f: I heard Xiaoyin's middle-school classmate Wang Hong is also coming.
content.side_g: 王 (wáng) is one of the most common Chinese surnames, originally meaning 'king.' 红 (hóng) means 'red,' a common given name for women evoking fortune and warmth. In the textbook narrative, 王红 is the younger sister of 王朋 (Wang Peng) and a classmate of 高小音. Family name comes first; the full name is 王红, not 红王.
